name: white bean zaatar salad
instructions:
Bloom whole cumin, coriander, and fennel seeds (and za'atar + aleppo pepper) in olive oil over medium heat until fragrant — ideally the spices are in whole seed form rather than ground. Add microplaned garlic and sauté briefly.

Pour the warm spiced oil directly over RAW white beans (not cooked-in-the-pan beans).

Fold in finely chopped fennel bulb and dill. Add lemon juice and lemon zest. Season with salt and black pepper to taste.

Finish with a generous sprinkle of dukkah on top.
notes:
From Nisha Vora (Big Vegan Flavor cookbook).

BLOOM THE SPICES IN OIL: the whole-seed bloom is the technique that gives this dish its identity. Toasting cumin, coriander, fennel seeds, za'atar, and aleppo pepper in warm oil releases the volatile aromatic compounds in a way that pre-ground spices can't deliver. Use whole seeds where possible; ground works but reads flatter.

WHITE BEANS STAY RAW (NO PAN): the warm spiced oil is poured OVER the beans rather than cooking them in the pan. Same principle as the french lentil salad — beans stay cool, the dish stays salad-shaped rather than stew-shaped. Heating the beans in the pan would push the dish toward a different category (a warm bean stew that would want stock and longer cook time).

FENNEL BULB + DILL: the anise character of the fennel bulb echoes the fennel seeds in the spice bloom; dill adds the eastern-mediterranean herbal lift. Both raw.

DUKKAH AS THE FINISH: the dukkah sprinkle gives the dish textural crunch (it's a nut-and-seed blend — see the homemade-dukkah recipe) and amplifies the spice profile already in the oil. Don't skip the dukkah — it's structural, not garnish.

LEMON ZEST IS LOAD-BEARING: zest carries more aromatic citrus oil than juice does. Use both, but the zest does more work in a dish this aromatic.
